title: Japanese milk bread
subtitle: Featherlight enriched loaf made with a tangzhong roux
excerpt: Japanese-style white bread made with a tangzhong — a cooked flour and milk paste that keeps the crumb extraordinarily soft and the crust thin. The result tears into featherlight layers. Makes one loaf.
sourceNotes: Shokupan is a Japanese Meiji-era bread tradition, adapted from Western enriched-bread methods in the late nineteenth century and refined through the twentieth century. The tangzhong technique (cooking a small portion of flour and liquid to a thick paste before adding to the dough) is documented in Asian baking guides from the 1990s onwards, where it is used to retain moisture and produce a uniquely soft crumb. No single public-domain Western text predating the twentieth century documents this specific technique in a bread context.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice before freezing. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be refrigerated after the bulk ferment for up to 16 hours. Bring to room temperature for 1 hour before shaping.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 190°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: A water roux made by cooking a small portion of flour and liquid together until the starch granules gelatinise and the mixture thickens to a paste. When added to bread dough, the pre-gelatinised starch can hold a larger amount of water than raw starch, resulting in a softer, more moist crumb that stays fresh longer. The technique originates in Japanese and Chinese baking and is used in enriched breads where extreme softness is the goal.

content.text: is made first and cooled before going into the dough. It is a cooked paste of flour and milk that holds additional moisture in the finished crumb. The result is a bread that tears into pillowy layers. It stays soft for two days without toasting, which is unusual for a homemade white loaf.

Whisk together the {{strong-bread-flour}} (tangzhong amount) and {{whole-milk}} (tangzhong amount) in a small saucepan. Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, until the mixture thickens to a smooth, gelatinous paste that pulls away from the sides of the pan, about 3–4 minutes. Scrape into a bowl and cool to room temperature.
Make the dough and bulk ferment — 90 minutes

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}} (dough amount), {{caster-sugar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{yeast-dried}} in a large bowl. Add the cooled tangzhong, {{whole-milk}} (dough amount), and {{eggs}} (one). Mix to a rough dough, then knead for 10 minutes. Add the softened {{unsalted-butter}} piece by piece and knead for a further 8 minutes until smooth and elastic. Cover and leave for 90 minutes until doubled.

Divide the dough into four equal pieces. Flatten each, roll into a rough rectangle, then roll up into a cylinder. Place side by side in a greased 900 g loaf tin. Cover and prove for 60 minutes until the dough has risen to the rim of the tin. Brush with beaten egg. Bake at 180°C fan for 30–35 minutes, until deep amber and the base sounds hollow when tapped. Cool on a rack for at least 1 hour before slicing.
